findings.title: Assistant-controlled markers can exfiltrate arbitrary host files to Discord
findings.description: The new attachment flow trusts any `<!--pidory:attach:...-->` marker found in assistant text and then reads that path from the bot host before uploading it to Discord. There is no allowlist, workspace boundary, or user confirmation step. Because the marker source is model output, a prompt injection or a direct user request can make the bot post readable secrets such as `.env`, SSH keys, or database files. `canonicalize()` only resolves the path; it does not enforce safety.
findings.suggestion: Do not treat plain assistant text as authority to read local files. Restrict attachments to an explicit safe root (for example the current workspace), reject absolute paths and `..` escapes outside that root, and preferably require a structured/signed tool result or explicit user approval before any host file is uploaded.

findings.title: Unbounded reads of non-regular files can hang or exhaust memory
findings.description: The implementation uses `metadata.len()` as the only size gate and then calls `tokio::fs::read(&canonical)`, which reads until EOF. That is unsafe for device files, FIFOs, and procfs/sysfs entries where `len()` is meaningless or zero. Paths like `/dev/zero` or certain `/proc` files can block forever or cause unbounded allocation, so a crafted marker can turn this feature into a DoS vector.
findings.suggestion: Reject anything that is not a regular file (`metadata.file_type().is_file()`), open the file first, and read through a bounded reader (`take(MAX_FILE_SIZE + 1)`) so the limit is enforced by the read itself instead of trusting metadata.

findings.title: Echoing raw filenames and paths into Discord content allows unintended mentions
findings.description: Success and error messages embed `filename`/`path` directly into message content, and the created messages do not disable mentions. A filename or requested path containing `@everyone`, `<@user>`, or similar mention syntax will ping users when the bot posts attachment status or failure text. Discord's docs explicitly recommend sanitizing user-generated strings and using `allowed_mentions` when echoing them back.
findings.suggestion: Set `allowed_mentions` to an empty parse set on these messages and escape or sanitize echoed filenames/paths before inserting them into Discord content.

summary: The attachment feature is not safe to ship as-is: it gives model output a direct file-upload primitive and performs unsafe reads on arbitrary host paths.
